feature_name: Dual operation modes: Root Complex (RC) and End Point (EP)
description: The controller supports operation as either a Root Complex or an End Point, selectable via PCIESSMODE[1:0] at boot with software override. In EP mode it can enumerate as a legacy or native PCIe endpoint, while RC mode exposes bridge-oriented registers and behaviors. Mode selection determines the configuration header type (Type 0 for EP and Type 1 for RC) and which capability registers are applicable. After mode selection and SerDes bring-up, software enables link training through CMD_STATUS.LTSSM_EN. This sequence ensures the link trains with the correct role and header semantics before any configuration or data traffic.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features; Section 1.4.1 PCI Express Core Module; Section 2.11 Operations
dependencies: Bootstraps PCIESSMODE[1:0]; CMD_STATUS.LTSSM_EN
Remarks: Choose mode early in initialization and do not change it after enumeration begins; verify header type usage; gate link training until SerDes is locked.

feature_name: Single link up to x2 with dynamic width, auto lane reversal and polarity inversion
description: A single bidirectional link supports widths up to x2 with automatic width negotiation during training. The controller supports dynamic-width conversion and automatic lane reversal to tolerate lane crossing on the PCB. RX polarity inversion is available, allowing easier routing without external crossovers. Negotiated link width and lane reversal status are observable in capability and debug registers for validation. These features reduce board constraints while maintaining compliance and interoperability.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features; LINK_CAP[9:4] (3.7.5); DEBUG1[0] lane reversal (3.9.12)
dependencies: SerDes lanes configured; PL_LINK_CTRL.LNK_MODE; LTSSM training complete
Remarks: Plan differential pair lengths per Gen2 requirements; rely on auto lane reversal and polarity inversion when routing forces swaps; confirm negotiated width via LINK_CAP and DEBUG registers.

feature_name: Gen1/Gen2 line rates and Gen2 directed speed change
description: The PHY supports 2.5 and 5.0 Gbps per-lane link speeds, with a constant 2.5 GHz PLL output and bus width conversion used for Gen2. Software can request a directed speed change to Gen2 after a stable Gen1 link using PL_GEN2.DIR_SPD. De-emphasis and TX swing are programmable to optimize for channel characteristics and compliance margins. Common-clock usage must be declared via LINK_STAT_CTRL.COMMON_CLK_CFG, especially with SSC backplanes. These controls allow predictable bring-up and reliable high-speed operation across varied channel conditions.
evidence: Section 2.3.3 Reference Clock Multiplication; PL_GEN2 (3.9.13); LINK_STAT_CTRL (3.7.6)
dependencies: SerDes PLL locked (PCIE_SERDES_STS.LOCK); PL_GEN2.DIR_SPD; LINK_STAT_CTRL.COMMON_CLK_CFG
Remarks: Establish Gen1 first, then change to Gen2; validate de-emphasis and swing against eye mask; use common clock settings when the platform shares REFCLK.

feature_name: Address Space 0/1 mapping and configuration windows
description: The subsystem presents two internal address spaces for software: Address Space 0 for application and configuration windows, and Address Space 1 for data movement. Address Space 0 is partitioned into four 4 KB regions covering application registers, local configuration, remote configuration, and an RC-only IO window. BAR0 is always mapped to the application register space so an RC can manage an EP even without EP firmware. Local configuration appears at a 0x1000 offset within Address Space 0 for standardized access. Software must avoid transactions to remote config/IO windows when the PCIe link is down to prevent timeouts.
evidence: Section 2.8 PCIe Address Spaces; Figure 2-5; Section 2.8.1.1 (Caution)
dependencies: BAR0 decode; CFG windows; Link up (DEBUG0.LTSSM_STATE==0x11)
Remarks: Mark these regions as non-cacheable and use single 32-bit accesses; guard all remote configuration and IO accesses behind link-up checks.

feature_name: Outbound address translation (32 regions)
description: Outbound translation maps internal addresses to PCIe addresses across 32 equally sized regions, each 1/2/4/8 MB as set by OB_SIZE. Region index is extracted from the internal address and the translated base is built from OB_OFFSET_INDEXn/OB_OFFSETn_HI. Software enables translation via CMD_STATUS.OB_XLT_EN only after programming all required regions. Transactions that span a region boundary are not automatically continued and must be split by software or DMA. Proper sizing and alignment of regions ensures deterministic TLP addressing without undefined behavior.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS.OB_XLT_EN=1; Program OB_* registers; Proper region size selection
Remarks: Map high-bandwidth buffers into larger regions and keep bursts within a single region; verify 64-bit translations with OB_OFFSETn_HI where required.

feature_name: Inbound address translation (4 regions) and BAR association
description: Inbound requests are first filtered by BAR matches and then translated to internal addresses using up to four regions. Each region selects a BAR with IB_BARn and defines PCIe-start and internal-offset via IB_STARTn_{LO,HI} and IB_OFFSETn. BAR0 is reserved and always decodes to application registers, and cannot be remapped by inbound translation. An optional BAR1 shortcut allows using the BAR1 value as the inbound start address if the IB_START for that region is zero. This scheme provides flexible host-to-device mapping while preserving a fixed control aperture for management.
evidence: Section 2.7.2; IB_* registers (3.1.82–3.1.97); BAR0 exception (2.7.2.2); Using BAR1 value (2.7.2.3)
dependencies: CMD_STATUS.IB_XLT_EN=1; Proper BAR programming; IB_* region setup
Remarks: Use ascending start addresses to map non-contiguous host ranges; ensure requests honor 4 KB crossing rules and translation windows.

feature_name: BAR Mask registers and DBI_CS2 access
description: BAR masks are write-only overlays that let firmware predefine BAR size and type prior to host enumeration. Writes are allowed only when CMD_STATUS.DBI_CS2 is set, and software must clear DBI_CS2 afterward, confirming completion with a read. BAR0 mask is limited because BAR0 is dedicated to application registers in both RC and EP modes. Mask programming is not visible through normal reads; use a test write/read pattern on the BAR to verify the effect. This mechanism allows EP self-sizing while preventing host-visible instability during discovery.
evidence: Section 2.7.3.1 BAR Mask Registers; CMD_STATUS.DBI_CS2 (3.1.3); BARx_MASK tables (3.3, 3.4)
dependencies: CMD_STATUS.DBI_CS2 handshake; BAR enabled; Link not required
Remarks: Do not modify masks over the serial link; validate mask effects by probing BAR mirror bits with safe test values before enumeration.

feature_name: DMA and data movement via master/slave VBUSM ports
description: A 128-bit slave VBUSM port serves outbound PCIe reads/writes driven by an SoC DMA, and a 128-bit master VBUSM port services inbound TLPs to internal memory. The documentation provides EDMA examples that chunk transfers to the configured PCIe payload size for efficiency. Expected sustainable bandwidth is about 85% of nominal PCIe throughput per lane and speed, assuming interconnect headroom. Address translation integrates with DMA to target remote or local address spaces consistently. Correct descriptor alignment and payload tuning minimize splits and maximize bus utilization.
dependencies: Configured address translation; EDMA setup; Adequate interconnect bandwidth
Remarks: Match EDMA aCnt to payload and bCnt to buffer length; maintain 128-byte boundaries to avoid master splits; verify interconnect QoS for both directions.

feature_name: Endian modes and swizzle for inbound/outbound data
description: The link is little-endian, but the subsystem supports big-endian SoCs by applying programmable byte-lane swaps. ENDIAN[1:0] selects swap on 1-, 2-, 4-, or 8-byte boundaries and applies symmetrically to data and byte enables. Inbound and outbound mapping tables specify exact lane relocation to preserve word semantics. Software should configure ENDIAN before enabling traffic to avoid mixed ordering in buffers. Pattern tests across 64-bit words validate correct endianness at system integration time.
dependencies: System endianness configuration; Program ENDIAN before data traffic
Remarks: Use mode A for byte swap, B for half-word, C for word, and D for double-word; verify with walking-1/0 patterns over burst transfers.

feature_name: Legacy INTx: generation (EP) and reception (RC)
description: An EP can synthesize legacy INTA–INTD messages by setting EP_IRQ_SET and later clearing with EP_IRQ_CLR to deassert. RC mode collects legacy interrupt messages, latches status per line, and requires proper IRQ_EOI writes to finish servicing. Only one legacy interrupt can be pending per EP function until deassert is observed by the RC. The messaging nature introduces race conditions if EOI precedes message deassert arrival. Drivers should probe downstream devices to confirm source and deassert state before issuing EOI to avoid re-trigger.
dependencies: Enable LEGACY_X IRQ; Proper EOI sequencing; Link up
Remarks: Prefer MSI where available to avoid legacy delivery ambiguities; if using INTx, enforce strict EOI ordering and readback synchronization.

feature_name: MSI: generation and reception with vectorized mapping
description: Both RC and EP support up to 32 MSI vectors with capability, address, and data registers defining delivery. EPs generate MSI by writing the configured MSI address/data or by writing RC’s MSI_IRQ register with the desired vector. On reception, MSIn_IRQ_STATUS bits latch events and are cleared with W1C followed by IRQ_EOI to complete handling. MSI ordering can be affected by relaxed ordering and posted-write behavior on the fabric. Software should coordinate memory completion visibility with MSI generation to avoid ISR accessing stale data.
evidence: Section 2.14.2.2; 2.14.5.2; MSI_CAP/LOW32/UP32/DATA (3.6.2–3.6.5); MSI_IRQ and MSI status blocks (3.1.16, 3.1.24–3.1.55)
dependencies: MSI_CAP.MSI_EN=1; Program MSI LOW/UP32 & DATA; Enable MSIn IRQ
Remarks: If data writes precede MSI, issue a read flush or fence on the RC side; allocate vectors per function and document ISR mapping clearly.

feature_name: Interrupt groups: MSI, Legacy, Error, PM/Reset mapping to 14 events
description: Fourteen interrupt event lines are exposed to the SoC, grouping MSI vectors, legacy INTx (RC-only), error classes, and PM/reset events. Each group provides RAW/EN/STATUS/CLR registers with W1S and W1C semantics to manage event lifecycles. System error aggregation includes fatal, non-fatal, correctable, AXI bridge fatal, and advanced error notifications. PM/reset events signal PME, PM turnoff/ack, and link reset requests for orderly transitions. Clear the appropriate STATUS before writing EOI to avoid retrigger and to maintain deterministic servicing.
evidence: Table 2-10 PCIESS Interrupt Events; ERR_IRQ_* (3.1.72–3.1.75); PMRST_* (3.1.76–3.1.79); IRQ_EOI (3.1.15)
dependencies: Enable bits per group; Proper W1C and EOI usage; Link/event source ready
Remarks: Map event lines to CPU IRQs with priority for errors; initialize masks at probe and audit status on ISR entry to deglitch spurious conditions.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting (AER) and root error handling
description: AER provides granular uncorrectable and correctable error status, masks, and severity control registers. First Error Pointer and four header log DWORDs capture the offending TLP header for post-mortem analysis. Root ports can be configured to raise system errors (SERR) on correctable, non-fatal, or fatal conditions and to generate PME interrupts. Software should enable only the messages relevant to system policy and mask benign conditions. When linking with devices that do not support ECRC, disable ECRC generation/check to prevent false error signaling.
dependencies: Enable DEV_STAT_CTRL error reporting and ROOT_ERR_CMD; Configure masks/severity
Remarks: Record header logs on first error and correlate with bus analyzer traces; classify and quarantine endpoints showing recurring non-fatal errors.

feature_name: Power management: D-states and ASPM L0s/L1
description: The device implements PCIe D-states (D0/D1/D2/D3hot/cold) and link ASPM states (L0s/L1), but does not support L2. PMCAP and PM_CTL_STAT control PME capabilities and entry/exit behavior, while LINK_STAT_CTRL and ACK_FREQ adjust link power-state transitions. Device and link states must be coordinated because transitions in one constrain the other. Software-driven moves to D1/D2/D3hot should quiesce traffic and respect configuration access rules. These mechanisms enable energy savings without sacrificing link reliability when properly tuned.
dependencies: PMCAP/PM_CTL_STAT fields; LINK_STAT_CTRL.ACTIVE_LINK_PM; ACK_FREQ programming
Remarks: Disable ASPM during bring-up, then enable incrementally; test wake and resume paths under load; document that L2 state is not supported.

feature_name: Reset mechanisms: POR/System reset and Software Hot Reset
description: Power-on and system resets reinitialize the entire PCIESS, with sticky fields retained across soft reset. Software can issue a downstream hot reset using RSTCMD.INIT_RST, after ensuring outbound and inbound traffic are drained. A minimum wait of 100 ms is required after reset before attempting any PCIe transaction, with up to 1.5 s tolerance if devices do not respond. PMRST interrupt status helps detect link reset requests and PM messages that can precede resets. Following reset, software must reconfigure the module and retrain the link before resuming traffic.
dependencies: Ensure traffic quiesced (ACT_STATUS); Respect post-reset delays; Re-init link via CMD_STATUS.LTSSM_EN
Remarks: Assert LTSSM disable during hot reset handling, reprogram BARs and translations as needed, and verify L0 state via DEBUG registers before DMA restarts.

feature_name: PIPE and PHY loopback for validation
description: PIPE loopback allows master/slave symbol-level test patterns between two devices, controlled by PL_LINK_CTRL.LPBK_EN. PHY loopback returns transmitted data to the receiver internally, enabling TLP path testing even without a link partner. PHY loopback setup requires programming SERDES_CFGx loopback fields and forcing the LTSSM state using PL_FORCE_LINK when needed. Loopback should be configured before any traffic to avoid sequence-number mismatches and replay drops. These modes are intended for validation and compliance testing rather than normal operation.
dependencies: SerDes configured and PLL locked; Loopback bits set; Force link to POLL_ACTIVE for PHY loopback
Remarks: Avoid addressing BAR0 in PHY loopback, skip configuration-type TLPs, and restore normal settings after tests to re-enter L0 cleanly.

feature_name: Clocking and SerDes PLL control
description: A high-quality differential REFCLK feeds the SerDes PLL, which generates PHY bit and functional clocks. PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL controls multiplication, loop bandwidth, sleep, and VCO range, and PCIE_SERDES_STS.LOCK indicates a stable output. The PCIe subsystem registers are only reliable after PLL lock is achieved. For platforms using common clocks and SSC, set LINK_STAT_CTRL.COMMON_CLK_CFG to optimize training sequences. Proper PLL configuration and lock verification are prerequisites to enabling the LTSSM and any link traffic.
dependencies: Stable refclk; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL.ENPLL=1; PCIE_SERDES_STS.LOCK=1; LINK_STAT_CTRL.COMMON_CLK_CFG
Remarks: Match LB settings to refclk quality; allow regulator settle time before lock polling; never access PCIESS clocks/registers before LOCK=1.

feature_name: LTSSM state visibility and debug registers
description: DEBUG0 and DEBUG1 provide visibility into training, PIPE signaling, and the current LTSSM state encoding. Appendix A maps the encoded values to state names such as POLL_ACTIVE, L0, and L0s for quick diagnostics. These registers also expose lane reversal detection, electrical idle, and transmitted ordered sets to aid bring-up. Regular polling during bring-up helps pinpoint negotiation stalls and partner misconfigurations. This observability shortens time-to-link by directing targeted configuration fixes.
dependencies: Accessible via Address Space 0 Application Registers; Link active for meaningful values
Remarks: Integrate LTSSM polling into bring-up scripts and log key transitions; correlate with analyzer traces for comprehensive root cause analysis.

feature_name: Transaction and alignment requirements
description: Outbound maximum payload size is 128 bytes, inbound maximum payload size is 256 bytes, and remote read requests may be up to 256 bytes. Write byte enables must be a single contiguous run of ones with no holes for correctness at the internal bus. Only incremental bursts are supported; fixed or wrap bursts can cause bus lockups. Misaligned outbound transactions larger than 120 bytes may exhibit unspecified behavior and must be split. Keeping command sizes at or below configured maxima prevents read interleaving and ensures predictable completions.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features; Section 2.10.1 (Bus Mastering, Alignment, Burst, Read Interleaving); TLPCFG (3.1.6)
dependencies: DEV_STAT_CTRL.MAX_PAYLD & MAX_REQ_SZ; TLPCFG.RELAXED/NO_SNOOP; Proper buffer alignment
Remarks: Align DMA descriptors to 128-byte boundaries, cap misaligned bursts to 120 bytes, and prohibit non-incremental bursts in platform interconnect policies.

feature_name: Configuration access: local/remote and IO window (RC)
description: RC mode performs Type 0/1 configuration using CFG_SETUP to set bus/device/function and type before issuing accesses. A 4 KB IO window in Address Space 0 converts internal accesses into IO TLPs with the IOBASE register providing high address bits. Local configuration registers are memory mapped at a 0x1000 offset within Address Space 0 for uniform access. Software must gate all remote configuration and IO operations until the link is trained to L0. This approach centralizes control-plane access while isolating it from data-plane regions.
evidence: Section 2.11.1.2; 2.8.1; CFG_SETUP (3.1.4); IOBASE (3.1.5); Config common/type regs (3.2–3.4)
dependencies: Program CFG_SETUP (bus/dev/fn/type); IOBASE[31:12]; Link up
Remarks: Limit configuration to 32-bit single accesses; handle config retries and completion timeouts per spec; avoid remote windows during link retrain.

feature_name: ACK/Replay timing and filter controls
description: Port Logic provides ACK frequency control, NFTS counts for L0s exit, and independent modifiers for ACK/Nak latency and Replay timers. SYM_NUM and SYMTIMER_FLTMASK expose additional knobs such as Flow Control watchdog and SKP interval to fine-tune robustness. Defaults are spec-compliant and should be left unchanged unless interoperability data justifies adjustment. Any changes require validation with SSC, common-clock, and worst-case channel conditions. Thoughtful tuning can improve recovery margins without harming interoperability.
dependencies: Stable link; Understanding of partner timing; Test coverage for modified values
Remarks: Document deviations from defaults and include in compliance reports; verify with BER, recovery, and LTSSM transition tests across temperature and voltage.

feature_name: Security features
description: The specification does not define security primitives such as access control, authentication, or link encryption. Error reporting and protocol checks enhance robustness but are not security mechanisms and should not be treated as such. BAR0 always exposes application registers to an RC, which is intended for manageability, not isolation. Consequently, platform-level measures like IOMMU/MPU, secure boot, and privilege separation must provide protection. Treat BAR sizing and translation windows as least-privilege surfaces and restrict mappings to only what software requires.
dependencies: UNKNOWN
Remarks: Harden access with interconnect firewalls and IOMMU policies; minimize BAR apertures; disable unused inbound regions; audit configuration from secure firmware.
